subject: Computer Networking
title: Email: An Accidental Killer App
description: A short lesson to help you apply Email: An Accidental Killer App.

hook.text: ARPANET was built for sharing computing resources. Instead, people used it to send each other messages.

buildup.text: In 1971, Ray Tomlinson wrote a program to send text between machines. He chose '@' to separate user from host.

discovery.text: Email exploded in popularity. Within two years, it accounted for 75% of all ARPANET traffic.

twist.text: Nobody planned for email to dominate. The network was designed for remote computing, not personal messages.

climax.text: Email proved that networks are most valuable for communication — a lesson that shaped every future network.

punchline.text: Email wasn't the plan. It became the point.

quiz.question: Who introduced the '@' symbol for email addresses?
